docs.text: About me Hi, I'm Nell. I'm a Senior Technical Writer for Accenture on contract with Google. I write documentation for Google Play's 2+ billion users and manage a team of 5 globally distributed writers. Specifically, I write and edit public-facing help documentation, internal knowledge base guides, and agent-facing content like announcements and canned responses. This site includes sample documentation I've written. The details My job also has several aspects beyond writing: I manage projects and people. As a writer, I manage projects from initial documentation design through post-publish Quality Assurance. I meet with stakeholders through the process to clarify project requirements and address issues. As a manager, I assign projects to my team and guide them through project life cycles. I edit in collaboration with cross-functional teams. I work with content strategists, product managers, legal teams, and usability experts to edit both public-facing and agent-facing documentation. I love balancing each team's needs with finding the right language to describe what an application actually does. I design content. I build the systems that make content possible. This might mean creating a style guide for the team or writing a template for project requirements. My Information Science degree reflects my love of making information searchable; I am your go-to person to fix an article's structure with the right information architecture or add metadata before publication. I consider how content will hold up over time and how to design it so it stays useful. I'm passionate about usability and accessibility. I test documentation and procedures by actually using them. Some days I review SOPs to find what's confusing. Others, I collaborate with engineers to improve a new project management software before rollout. I have previous professional experience in making content accessible for users with disabilities, which is often useful in my current work. I'm an AI trainer and editor. I train AI systems to generate documentation on a topic through prompt engineering, then edit the output to align with the team's style guide and standards. I hope this description gives you a fuller picture of my work than a resume can capture, though you'll find the obligatory resume below. History I grew up in Los Angeles, went to college in Ohio, and majored in English. Though I went to graduate school to become a corporate researcher, writing has always been my favorite part of work; eventually I became a professional writer. But "no knowledge is wasted," and I still organize information as a technical writer. Nell Gram Resume Location: Remote (currently in Los Angeles, relocating to the East Coast) Portfolio Help Center article Knowledge Base article Release notes Contact Email: nellcgram@gmail.com LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/nellgram GitHub: Nell C Gram

docs.text: What Is an EOB? An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a document your health insurance company sends after you receive medical care. It is not a bill. It shows what your doctor charged, what your insurance paid, and what you may owe. What You Will Find in This Document This guide explains each section of your EOB in plain language so you know exactly what you're looking at. How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits An EOB document contains your name, address, member ID, and insurance group number. It also lists the following information: Key Numbers on EOB Billed Amount The original total charged for the medical service. Allowed Amount The amount your insurance agrees to pay based on your plan's coverage Insurance Payment The portion of the allowed amount that your insurance covered Amount Owed The amount you're responsible for that remains after insurance pays. This includes your deductible, copay, and coinsurance. In-Network and Out-of-Network Costs In-network providers: Find "network savings" box to see what you saved. Out-of-network providers: You are responsible for any difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount, and the amount you owe may be higher than expected. Check the network savings line. If it shows $0, you used an out-of-network provider. Remark Codes Usually 2 or 3 letters or numbers. Check the EOB bottom for a description of the code. The description is a note from the health plan that explains details about the costs. How to Check for Errors Errors on EOBs are common. Checking yours can save you money. Check the date and service. Did you receive this care on this date? Compare your EOB to your doctor's bill. The amount you owe should match. Check the network status. Did you see an in-network doctor? Your network savings should not show $0. Look up any remark codes. The descriptions are at the bottom of your EOB. If something looks wrong, call your doctor's billing office first. Then call your insurance company if needed.
docs.title: EOB Guide

docs.text: Help Center Article How to use Story Board view in Scribbler View sample About I created this sample Help Center article for a fictional writing application called Scribbler, styled after documentation conventions used for Google Play. Why I chose this project Rather than document the actual Scrivener application, I opted to fictionalize the content, creating a similar interface with comparable terminology. The focus remained narrowly on the Story Board view for project outlining rather than covering the entire Scribbler application. I deliberately addressed customization features like color coding and metadata creation to demonstrate information organization and discoverability. Content strategy This article includes IA annotations showing a content model used for single-source publishing in a Content Management System. The structure follows a Help Center "How to / About" model beginning with benefits, progressing through tasks and tips, and concluding with related resources. Organization moves from basic to advanced customization options. Writing and design approach Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: friendly, empathetic, conversational Clarity: 7th grade reading level, active voice, front-loaded sentences Formatting: Sentence case for titles, title case for UI, breadcrumb navigation Design decisions: I differentiated between HTML tag usage for visual hierarchy and accessibility: kbd tags for keyboard shortcuts, code tags for UI elements, and strong tags for conceptual emphasis. Two distinct callout boxes were created: blue callout boxes for informational context and yellow boxes for warnings.
docs.title: Help Center Article

docs.text: Internal Knowledge Base Article Agent guide to processing loan extensions View sample Project overview I created this sample internal Knowledge Base article for library circulation staff, drawing from publicly-available Los Angeles Public Library policies. It demonstrates agent-facing documentation designed to support customer service teams. Content strategy Organization: The article prioritizes agent workflows, sequencing from routine tasks (renewals) through uncommon scenarios (lost cards, damaged items) for efficient navigation. Scripted responses: Templated answers and canned responses provide starting frameworks for customer interactions, similar to support resources developed for Google teams. Writing and design approach Visual hierarchy for reference: HTML code tags for interface elements Expandable sections to minimize cognitive burden Color-coded reference boxes highlighting critical information Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: conversational, empathetic, welcoming Clarity: accessible reading level, active voice, prioritized information Formatting: sentence case headers, proper capitalization for UI elements, navigation breadcrumbs Responses emphasize customer empathy Key features Task-based workflows for typical circulation scenarios Eligibility reference table for rapid policy verification Template email and chat responses with customizable placeholders Metrics reporting guidance for escalation decisions
docs.title: KB Article

docs.text: Release Notes eReader App Monthly Updates View sample About I created sample release notes for a fictional eReader application, modeled after the style used for Google Play releases. See also: Release notes for Google Play Games on PC My approach Rather than presenting a technical changelog, these notes prioritize user benefits. The strategy aims to minimize support requests and encourage adoption of new capabilities. Key elements User value first: Emphasis on user benefit alongside new features — for example, "Adds a streak tracker that shows how many days you've read in a row," rather than "Implements streak tracking functionality" Action-oriented language: Descriptions help users understand how to use updated app functions Balanced scope: A combination of significant features and performance enhancements to set appropriate user expectations
docs.title: Release Notes
